title: DSDS Criterion Definitions
description: Shared definitions for testable success criteria, conformance, and external standard references. Criteria are the test-definition layer of DSDS documentation: each pairs a stable identifier with an objectively verifiable statement, an optional conformance `level`, a `verification` mode (automated, assisted, manual, or custom), and an optional machine-dispatchable `check`. Fixtures with declared outcomes make criteria self-verifying. Used by guideline entries and accessibility blocks. Observed results belong in a guideline entry's `evidence`, not here.

conformanceLevel.description: Conformance level, named for RFC 2119 requirement levels: 'must' and 'must-not' are absolute requirements (non-compliance is a defect); 'should' and 'should-not' are strong defaults that valid exceptions may override when the implications are understood. Values are lowercase kebab-case like every DSDS-native vocabulary â€” in prose, the keywords stay capitalized (MUST, SHOULD NOT) where they carry conformance weight. Shared by guideline entries and criteria so reports aggregate on one severity vocabulary.

verificationMode.description: How a result for this criterion is produced â€” who decides the outcome. 'automated': a runner fully decides; a `check` MUST be present. 'assisted': a tool surfaces candidates but a human decides; `check` diagnostics are advisory. 'manual': pure human judgment; no `check` applies â€” `techniques` and `failures` serve as the reviewer's procedure. This is a closed set: the axis is who decides, and named procedures (e.g. a design review) are the 'how', which belongs in `techniques`. When omitted, tools MUST NOT assume the criterion is automatable.

criterionCheck.description: A dispatch envelope for automated or assisted verification. DSDS defines only the envelope: `scheme` names the runner or rule system (open vocabulary, e.g. 'axe-core', 'vitest', 'stylelint', 'lighthouse'); all other properties are scheme-specific configuration owned by the named tool. A runner that does not recognize the scheme MUST report the criterion as skipped, never as passing. Like `extensions`, this object is intentionally open: execution semantics live in tools, not in this specification.

scheme.description: Identifier of the rule system or runner this check dispatches to. MUST be lowercase; dots permitted for namespacing (e.g. 'com.acme.contract-tests').

criterionFixture.description: An example with a declared outcome, used as a verification fixture for a criterion. Identical to `example` plus a required `outcome`. NOTE: this is an intentional copy of the `example` shape, not a composition â€” `example` is a closed schema (additionalProperties: false), so allOf-extension cannot add `outcome`. When `example` changes, change this definition to match (example.schema.json carries the mirror note). Fixtures make criteria self-verifying: a conformance runner SHOULD execute the criterion's `check` (when present) against each fixture and confirm the declared outcome is reproduced â€” a 'fail' fixture that passes means the check no longer detects what it claims to.

title.description: A human-readable caption for the example (e.g., 'Primary button in default state'). For API property examples, use `value` for the literal content and `title` for the display label.

description.description: An explanation of the example â€” why it is encouraged, discouraged, or notable.

value.description: A literal value carried by the example. Use it for API property examples, where the content is a concrete value rather than a visual demo. It can display code that reflects the example. Accepts any JSON type: string, number, boolean, object, array, or null. When given without a presentation, this value alone is the example. Do not use `value` for display text â€” use `title` for captions and `description` for explanations.

presentation.description: The visual or interactive presentation for the example. Required when no `value` is set. Optional when `value` is present. Value-only examples are concrete data points that tools render as table rows or inline displays.

outcome.description: Whether this fixture satisfies ('pass') or violates ('fail') the criterion. Replaces encoding pass/fail in `title` prose, which tools cannot rely on.

reference.description: A citation of an external standard or requirement. This is a published rule the docs address (WCAG, platform guidelines, vendor compliance frameworks).

url.description: URL to the external standard or requirement (e.g., 'https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/#contrast-minimum').

label.description: A display label for the citation (e.g., '1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum)', 'WCAG 2.5.8 Target Size'). When omitted, tools MAY build a label from the URL.

criterion.description: A testable success criterion: an objectively verifiable condition an implementation must meet. Criteria let documentation act as a test harness â€” a result is produced per `verification` mode and reported under the criterion's `identifier`. `statement` is the normative, human-readable source of truth; `check`, when present, is its machine-dispatchable shadow and never replaces it. Pair with `techniques` (how to pass), `failures` (what failing looks like), `references` (external standards), and `examples` (fixtures with declared outcomes).

identifier.description: Stable identifier for this criterion, unique within the parent entity (e.g., 'touch-target-minimum', 'single-primary-action'). MUST be lowercase kebab-case. Test runs report pass/fail against this identifier, so it SHOULD NOT change once checks depend on it â€” renaming breaks result history across runs.

title.description: A short display name for the criterion (e.g., 'Touch Target Minimum'). Tools SHOULD render it in docs and test reports beside the identifier.

statement.description: The testable statement of what success looks like. MUST be objectively verifiable â€” by inspection, static analysis, or runtime measurement. Avoid vague language like 'feels right' or 'looks balanced' (e.g., 'Interactive elements present a hit area of at least 44Ă—44 px').

techniques.description: Sufficient ways to satisfy this criterion â€” the passing patterns (e.g., 'Apply `min-height: 44px` to every interactive element', 'Use the `<Button>` component without overriding its default height').

failures.description: Known failure modes â€” conditions that count as failing this criterion (e.g., 'Setting `height` below 44px on an interactive element', 'Removing `aria-label` from an icon-only button').

references.description: External standards this criterion aligns with or derives from (e.g., a matching WCAG success criterion, a platform guideline).

examples.description: Verification fixtures â€” typically one passing and one failing implementation, each with a declared `outcome`.

tags.description: Freeform keywords that relate criteria across guidelines and entities (e.g., 'a11y', 'contrast', 'touch-target').

level.description: The conformance level of this criterion. A criterion referenced by a guideline SHOULD inherit the guideline's `level` when omitted; a standalone criterion SHOULD declare its own.

since.description: The design system version in which this criterion was introduced (e.g., '1.0.0', '2.3.0'). Criterion identifiers are stable: once published, an identifier MUST NOT be reused for a different requirement â€” deprecate and mint a new one instead.
